title: Anterior Uveitis / Iritis
oneLiner: Unilateral red, painful, photophobic eye from iris/ciliary body inflammation; common in young adults; sight-threatening if synechiae/glaucoma develop.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute angle-closure glaucoma:** severe pain, nausea, blurred vision, fixed mid-dilated pupil, IOP >40 mmHg, corneal oedema. Differentiator: IOP and pupil shape (mid-dilated/fixed vs small/irregular in iritis).
- **Endophthalmitis:** severe pain, hypopyon, vitreous haze, recent intraocular surgery or penetrating trauma. Differentiator: vitreous involvement (poor red reflex) and rapid onset.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Unilateral red eye
  - Deep, dull ocular pain (worse with eye movement)
  - Photophobia (especially on slit lamp)
  - Blurred vision (transient)
  - Possible floaters (if mild vitreous cells)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Circumcorneal injection (ciliary flush) — red ring at limbus
  - Small, irregular pupil (due to spasm + possible synechiae)
  - Cells and flare in anterior chamber on slit lamp
  - Possible hypopyon (layered white cells) — consider HLA-B27, Behçet's
  - Normal intraocular pressure (IOP) usually, but can be elevated
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Conjunctivitis:** bilateral, gritty, watery/mucoid discharge, no photophobia, no cells in anterior chamber
- **Keratitis (corneal ulcer):** severe photophobia, pain, fluorescein uptake, risk of perforation
- **Acute angle-closure glaucoma:** rock-hard eye, fixed mid-dilated pupil, high IOP, nausea
- **Scleritis:** deep boring pain, no photophobia, scleral vessels not mobile with phenylephrine, often autoimmune
- **Endophthalmitis:** post-op, severe, hypopyon, vitreous involvement
- **Splitter:** Fluorescein staining — if positive, think keratitis (do not give topical steroids!). If negative, think iritis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart)
  - Slit lamp examination (mandatory) — cells and flare grade
  - Intraocular pressure (Tonopen or Goldman)
  - Fluorescein stain (rule out corneal ulcer)
  - Pupil assessment (shape, reactivity, relative afferent defect)
- **Bloods:**
  - First episode: FBC, ESR, CRP (screen for systemic inflammation)
  - Recurrent or bilateral: HLA-B27, ANA, ACE, RF, syphilis serology, chest X-ray
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray if sarcoid suspected (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy)
  - Sacroiliac joints if ankylosing spondylitis suspected (clinically)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine dip for blood/protein (glomerulonephritis in SLE/vasculitis)
  - Joint examination for arthritis (HLA-B27 spondyloarthropathy)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - SUN working group classification: acute (≤6 weeks), chronic (>6 weeks), granulomatous vs non-granulomatous
  - Key threshold: cells ≥1+ on slit lamp = definite uveitis
- **Never-miss test:** Intraocular pressure measurement (to diagnose angle-closure glaucoma before giving mydriatics/steroids)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Topical steroids: prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops **hourly** while awake (or [check local policy] for dose/frequency)
  - Cycloplegic: cyclopentolate 1% drops TDS (or atropine 1% BD if severe synechiae)
  - Analgesia: simple analgesics (paracetamol, NSAIDs if no contraindication)
  - **Do NOT start steroids if fluorescein staining positive** — refer ophthalmology immediately
  - Urgent ophthalmology review (within 24h, same-day if hypopyon, IOP >30, or vision <6/12)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue topical steroids as per ophthalmology plan (taper based on response)
  - Monitor IOP daily if steroid started (risk of steroid-induced glaucoma)
  - If severe or bilateral: consider oral prednisolone 40-60mg daily (always with ophthalmology senior input)
  - Rheumatology referral if recurrent or HLA-B27 positive
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Taper topical steroids over weeks (e.g. hourly → 4 times daily → twice daily → stop)
  - Continue cycloplegic until synechiae broken and pain controlled (usually 1-2 weeks)
  - Recurrence prevention: treat underlying condition (e.g. sulphasalazine for spondyloarthropathy)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: maintenance IV if NBM (usually not needed)
  - VTE prophylaxis: only if immobile or high risk (not routine)
  - Diet: as tolerated
  - Observations: NEWS2 usually stable; only if systemic symptoms
  - **Eye shield if severe photophobia**
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops: **1 drop hourly** initially (waking hours only) — duration: until cells/flare resolve, then taper
  - Cyclopentolate 1% eye drops: **1 drop TDS** — shorter acting; if synechiae present → atropine 1% BD
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Dexamethasone 0.1% eye drops (if severe) — more potent, higher IOP risk
  - Homatropine 2% eye drops (if cyclopentolate insufficient)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Topical steroids contraindicated in herpetic keratitis (can perforate)
  - Cycloplegics contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma (worsen pupil block)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for topical drops. Oral steroids adjust if hepatic impairment (reduce dose, monitor glucose)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving topical steroids without excluding corneal ulcer or herpetic keratitis** — can cause rapid corneal perforation. Always check fluorescein first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Posterior synechiae (iris adhesion to lens) — causes irregular pupil, can lead to iris bombe → angle closure
  - Elevated IOP (either from inflammation or steroid response) — can cause glaucoma
  - Hypopyon (if severe)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cataract (steroid-induced or chronic inflammation)
  - Glaucoma (synechial angle closure or steroid response)
  - Band keratopathy (calcium deposition in cornea)
  - Cystoid macular oedema (if intermediate/posterior extension)
sections.Prognosis:
- Most acute episodes resolve fully with prompt treatment over 2-4 weeks.
- Recurrence rate ~30-50%, especially in HLA-B27+ patients (ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis).
- Vision loss rare if treated, but chronic/recurrent cases can lead to permanent blindness from glaucoma or maculopathy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure IOP before starting steroids** — if high, consider oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide) and urgent ophthalmology.
- **Use cycloplegics early** — they break and prevent posterior synechiae, reduce pain, and allow better examination.
- **If hypopyon present, think Behçet's or HLA-B27** — ask about oral/genital ulcers, arthritis, and refer for systemic workup.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe topical steroids without a slit lamp exam** — you might miss keratitis or a corneal abrasion.
- **Do not forget to dilate the pupil** — posterior synechiae can be hidden behind a miotic pupil.
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** — always taper to avoid rebound inflammation.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening pain (especially if associated with nausea/vomiting — think angle closure)
  - Sudden loss of vision or new floaters/flashes (vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment)
  - Eye becoming more red or discharge (superinfection)
- **Follow-up:** Ophthalmology clinic within 24-48 hours for IOP check and steroid taper plan.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ophthalmology registrar immediately if:**
  - IOP >30 mmHg despite treatment
  - Hypopyon with severe pain (exclude endophthalmitis)
  - Inability to examine fundus (no red reflex)
  - Visual acuity <6/60
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - Signs of systemic sepsis (fever, rigors) + hypopyon (consider endophthalmitis)
  - NEWS2 >5 (rare, but possible in severe systemic disease like Behçet's with ocular crisis)

rawMarkdown:
## Anterior Uveitis / Iritis
**One-liner:** Unilateral red, painful, photophobic eye from iris/ciliary body inflammation; common in young adults; sight-threatening if synechiae/glaucoma develop.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute angle-closure glaucoma:** severe pain, nausea, blurred vision, fixed mid-dilated pupil, IOP >40 mmHg, corneal oedema. Differentiator: IOP and pupil shape (mid-dilated/fixed vs small/irregular in iritis).
- **Endophthalmitis:** severe pain, hypopyon, vitreous haze, recent intraocular surgery or penetrating trauma. Differentiator: vitreous involvement (poor red reflex) and rapid onset.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Unilateral red eye
  - Deep, dull ocular pain (worse with eye movement)
  - Photophobia (especially on slit lamp)
  - Blurred vision (transient)
  - Possible floaters (if mild vitreous cells)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Circumcorneal injection (ciliary flush) — red ring at limbus
  - Small, irregular pupil (due to spasm + possible synechiae)
  - Cells and flare in anterior chamber on slit lamp
  - Possible hypopyon (layered white cells) — consider HLA-B27, Behçet's
  - Normal intraocular pressure (IOP) usually, but can be elevated

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Conjunctivitis:** bilateral, gritty, watery/mucoid discharge, no photophobia, no cells in anterior chamber
- **Keratitis (corneal ulcer):** severe photophobia, pain, fluorescein uptake, risk of perforation
- **Acute angle-closure glaucoma:** rock-hard eye, fixed mid-dilated pupil, high IOP, nausea
- **Scleritis:** deep boring pain, no photophobia, scleral vessels not mobile with phenylephrine, often autoimmune
- **Endophthalmitis:** post-op, severe, hypopyon, vitreous involvement
- **Splitter:** Fluorescein staining — if positive, think keratitis (do not give topical steroids!). If negative, think iritis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart)
  - Slit lamp examination (mandatory) — cells and flare grade
  - Intraocular pressure (Tonopen or Goldman)
  - Fluorescein stain (rule out corneal ulcer)
  - Pupil assessment (shape, reactivity, relative afferent defect)
- **Bloods:**
  - First episode: FBC, ESR, CRP (screen for systemic inflammation)
  - Recurrent or bilateral: HLA-B27, ANA, ACE, RF, syphilis serology, chest X-ray
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray if sarcoid suspected (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy)
  - Sacroiliac joints if ankylosing spondylitis suspected (clinically)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine dip for blood/protein (glomerulonephritis in SLE/vasculitis)
  - Joint examination for arthritis (HLA-B27 spondyloarthropathy)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - SUN working group classification: acute (≤6 weeks), chronic (>6 weeks), granulomatous vs non-granulomatous
  - Key threshold: cells ≥1+ on slit lamp = definite uveitis
- **Never-miss test:** Intraocular pressure measurement (to diagnose angle-closure glaucoma before giving mydriatics/steroids)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Topical steroids: prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops **hourly** while awake (or [check local policy] for dose/frequency)
  - Cycloplegic: cyclopentolate 1% drops TDS (or atropine 1% BD if severe synechiae)
  - Analgesia: simple analgesics (paracetamol, NSAIDs if no contraindication)
  - **Do NOT start steroids if fluorescein staining positive** — refer ophthalmology immediately
  - Urgent ophthalmology review (within 24h, same-day if hypopyon, IOP >30, or vision <6/12)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue topical steroids as per ophthalmology plan (taper based on response)
  - Monitor IOP daily if steroid started (risk of steroid-induced glaucoma)
  - If severe or bilateral: consider oral prednisolone 40-60mg daily (always with ophthalmology senior input)
  - Rheumatology referral if recurrent or HLA-B27 positive
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Taper topical steroids over weeks (e.g. hourly → 4 times daily → twice daily → stop)
  - Continue cycloplegic until synechiae broken and pain controlled (usually 1-2 weeks)
  - Recurrence prevention: treat underlying condition (e.g. sulphasalazine for spondyloarthropathy)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: maintenance IV if NBM (usually not needed)
  - VTE prophylaxis: only if immobile or high risk (not routine)
  - Diet: as tolerated
  - Observations: NEWS2 usually stable; only if systemic symptoms
  - **Eye shield if severe photophobia**

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops: **1 drop hourly** initially (waking hours only) — duration: until cells/flare resolve, then taper
  - Cyclopentolate 1% eye drops: **1 drop TDS** — shorter acting; if synechiae present → atropine 1% BD
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Dexamethasone 0.1% eye drops (if severe) — more potent, higher IOP risk
  - Homatropine 2% eye drops (if cyclopentolate insufficient)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Topical steroids contraindicated in herpetic keratitis (can perforate)
  - Cycloplegics contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma (worsen pupil block)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for topical drops. Oral steroids adjust if hepatic impairment (reduce dose, monitor glucose)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving topical steroids without excluding corneal ulcer or herpetic keratitis** — can cause rapid corneal perforation. Always check fluorescein first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Posterior synechiae (iris adhesion to lens) — causes irregular pupil, can lead to iris bombe → angle closure
  - Elevated IOP (either from inflammation or steroid response) — can cause glaucoma
  - Hypopyon (if severe)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cataract (steroid-induced or chronic inflammation)
  - Glaucoma (synechial angle closure or steroid response)
  - Band keratopathy (calcium deposition in cornea)
  - Cystoid macular oedema (if intermediate/posterior extension)

### Prognosis
- Most acute episodes resolve fully with prompt treatment over 2-4 weeks.
- Recurrence rate ~30-50%, especially in HLA-B27+ patients (ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis).
- Vision loss rare if treated, but chronic/recurrent cases can lead to permanent blindness from glaucoma or maculopathy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure IOP before starting steroids** — if high, consider oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide) and urgent ophthalmology.
- **Use cycloplegics early** — they break and prevent posterior synechiae, reduce pain, and allow better examination.
- **If hypopyon present, think Behçet's or HLA-B27** — ask about oral/genital ulcers, arthritis, and refer for systemic workup.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe topical steroids without a slit lamp exam** — you might miss keratitis or a corneal abrasion.
- **Do not forget to dilate the pupil** — posterior synechiae can be hidden behind a miotic pupil.
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** — always taper to avoid rebound inflammation.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening pain (especially if associated with nausea/vomiting — think angle closure)
  - Sudden loss of vision or new floaters/flashes (vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment)
  - Eye becoming more red or discharge (superinfection)
- **Follow-up:** Ophthalmology clinic within 24-48 hours for IOP check and steroid taper plan.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ophthalmology registrar immediately if:**
  - IOP >30 mmHg despite treatment
  - Hypopyon with severe pain (exclude endophthalmitis)
  - Inability to examine fundus (no red reflex)
  - Visual acuity <6/60
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - Signs of systemic sepsis (fever, rigors) + hypopyon (consider endophthalmitis)
  - NEWS2 >5 (rare, but possible in severe systemic disease like Behçet's with ocular crisis)
